cataloging_subject.label: The Book of Quinte Essence
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's Latin Liber de consideratione quintae essentiae, represented in the encoding by its two intrinsic books on edition pages 1-25. The record does not treat Hermes Trismegistus's inherited legendary attribution as modern authorship. It retains Furnivall's title page, introduction, 1889 contents, and C. H. Gill's chemistry notes as zero-count edition paratexts; it models The Spheres and Planets on page 26 as a separately appended related medieval work from Sloane MS 73, f. 26, not as Book III or an intrinsic section of The Book of Quinte Essence. The revised edition's glossary on pages 29-31 is listed in the encoded contents but absent from the repository XML.

agents.name: Hermes Trismegistus
alternate_names: Hermys þe prophete and kyng of Egipt
notes: Named in the Middle English incipit as the legendary recipient of revealed wisdom. Furnivall already cautioned that more works were credited to Hermes than he wrote; modern source scholarship identifies John of Rupescissa's Latin treatise behind this translation.

agents.name: Edmund Brock
notes: Furnivall states that Brock read the proof against Sloane MS 73.

agents.name: C. H. Gill
notes: Author of the separately headed nineteenth-century Notes on the Chemistry of the Text.

scope.description: Translation from John of Rupescissa's Latin treatise

assertion.notes: The Middle English incipit calls itself a treatise briefly drawn from a Latin book; no translator is named.
Halversen identifies Sloane MS 73 and Harley MS 853 as witnesses to a second, condensed English version that omits and rearranges material relative to the standard version.

scope.description: Inherited legendary source attribution in the incipit, not accepted modern authorship

assertion.notes: The attribution's presence is certain; its historical authorship claim is rejected.

scope.description: Editor of the Sloane MS 73 transcription in the 1866 and revised 1889 print states
Read the edition proof against Sloane MS 73
Nineteenth-century chemical commentary, not part of the medieval work

date_statements.display: Fifteenth century; before or by the Sloane MS 73 witness dated circa 1460-1470

assertion.notes: The normalized range is a cautious witness- and MED-based bound, not a demonstrated translation date.

date_statements.display: Circa 1460-1470 in British Library, Sloane MS 73, ff. 10r-25v

assertion.notes: Furnivall reports E. A. Bond's circa 1460-1470 dating. The current interim British Library record labels the item only fifteenth century and does not repeat that narrow date.
The repository encoding follows the revised textual state, not the unrevised 1866 state.

places.label: England
notes: Used only for the broad cultural and witness context; the exact place of translation and dialect region have not been established.

assertion.notes: Middle English language and the English Sloane witness support an English cultural context but not a more precise composition place.

form_statements.description: Continuous instructional and medical prose in two books

assertion.notes: Book II applies quintessence preparations to a sequence of illnesses and therapeutic aims.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English condensed translation and adaptation of John of Rupescissa's fourteenth-century Latin treatise on quintessence. Book I explains the theory and manufacture of a purified, repeatedly distilled substance associated with aqua ardens and describes extracting or combining quintessences from gold, antimony, blood, and other materials. Book II applies the substance to rejuvenation and remedies for leprosy, palsy, fevers, gout, frenzy, poison, pestilence, and other conditions.

The medieval work has two intrinsic books. The encoded carrier also contains a title page, Furnivall's introduction, contents added in 1889, the unrelated appended Spheres and Planets from the next Sloane folio, and C. H. Gill's modern chemical notes. The printed glossary listed in the contents is not encoded.

Hermes Trismegistus is retained only as a legendary attribution inherited by the incipit. The Latin source work is attributed to John of Rupescissa by modern scholarship; neither figure is converted into the author of the anonymous Middle English translation-adaptation. Edition paratexts and the separately appended astrological text are not counted as medieval-work items.

extent.description: One predominantly Middle English prose work in two intrinsic books on revised-edition pages 1-25, with brief embedded Latin citations and a Latin closing caution. The title page, introduction, contents, separately appended Spheres and Planets, and Gill notes are retained as zero-count parts; the edition's listed glossary is absent from the XML.

content_parts.label: Edition title page
content_parts.summary: Furnivall edition title and publication matter retained as print paratext.

content_parts.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's introduction
content_parts.summary: Editorial introduction dated 16 May 1866, with an 1889 postscript identifying newly added contents and Spheres material.

content_parts.label: Contents added in 1889
content_parts.summary: Two-page editorial contents list covering Books I-II, Spheres and Planets, Gill's notes, and the printed glossary.
notes: The contents list promises a glossary on page 29, but the XML stops after Gill's notes on page 28.

content_parts.label: The Book of Quinte Essence
content_parts.summary: The one cataloged Middle English prose translation-adaptation, divided into two books.

content_parts.label: Book I
content_parts.summary: Theory, preparation, and extraction of quintessence, including repeated distillation of wine and preparations involving gold, antimony, blood, and the four elements.

content_parts.label: Book II
content_parts.summary: Sixteen numbered medicines and additional therapeutic directions applying quintessence to rejuvenation, disease, poison, fevers, and pestilence.

content_parts.label: The Spheres and Planets
content_parts.summary: A separately appended Middle English astrological text transcribed from the following manuscript folio and added to the edition in 1889; it is not an intrinsic book of The Book of Quinte Essence.

content_parts.label: Notes on the Chemistry of the Text
content_parts.summary: C. H. Gill's nineteenth-century explanatory and critical notes on the treatise's chemistry.

related_works.title: Liber de consideratione quintae essentiae omnium rerum
notes: John of Rupescissa composed the two-book Latin source in 1351-1352. The Sloane/Harley English version is condensed and rearranged; no physical Latin exemplar for this translation has been identified.

related_works.title: The Spheres and Planets
notes: This short separate work follows the target in Sloane MS 73 at f. 26 and was appended to Furnivall's edition only in the 1889 revision. It is retained as part:spheres-and-planets with item_count zero.

evidence.citation: Pinned General Corpus XML for University of Michigan CME item ANZ4356
evidence.summary: Confirms the encoded title, edition statement, two-book work, incipit and explicit, page and folio milestones, separate Spheres section, Gill notes, and the contents-listed but unencoded glossary.

evidence.citation: Local structural audit of the pinned ANZ4356 XML
evidence.summary: Counts one title page, four DIV1 elements, three DIV2 elements, 68 TEXT-level P elements, 20 NOTE1 elements, 33 PB elements, and 34 manuscript-folio MILESTONE elements; identifies Books I-II as ANZ4356:3.1-3.2 and Spheres as ANZ4356:3.3.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Book of Quinte Essence, revised edition (1889), complete Wikimedia Commons scan
evidence.summary: Confirms the revised 1889 state, Furnivall's editorial statements, target text on pages 1-25, separately added Spheres on page 26, Gill notes on pages 27-28, and glossary on pages 29-31.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Book of Quinte Essence, first edition (1866), complete Internet Archive scan
evidence.locator: title page; target pp. 1-25; Gill notes and glossary following
evidence.summary: The inspected title page gives 1866, and the volume lacks the 1889 contents and Spheres additions. It establishes the earlier edition state independently of the provider's incorrect 1889 metadata date.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Sloane MS 73, record 040-002112411
evidence.summary: Confirms the target's fifteenth-century English item at ff. 10-25, the separate astrology item at f. 26, the holding institution, and the absence of linked digitised content in the current record.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 853, record 040-002046682
evidence.summary: Confirms the collateral English witness at ff. 66r-73v, its dated ending of 22 August 1589, and the absence of linked digitised content in the current record.

evidence.citation: Marguerite A. Halversen, The Consideration of Quintessence: An Edition of a Middle English Translation of John of Rupescissa's Liber de Consideratione de Quintae Essentiae Omnium Rerum, PhD diss., Michigan State University, 1998
evidence.summary: Identifies John of Rupescissa's 1351-1352 Latin source, the English version families, Sloane MS 73 and Harley MS 853 as the condensed second version, the omissions and rearrangements in that version, and Furnivall's edition history.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, source stencil a1475 Bk.Quint.(Sln 73)
evidence.summary: Confirms the MED's a1475 source dating and abbreviated title for Furnivall's Sloane MS 73 text.

open_questions.question: When and where was this condensed Middle English translation-adaptation made, what dialect does it represent, and what Latin exemplar did its translator use?
next_steps: Consult Halversen's full textual analysis and later scholarship on the English version families.
Seek dialectal analysis and any proposed Latin exemplar or intermediary.

open_questions.question: Why does Halversen describe the Sloane text as ff. 11-25v while Furnivall's folio milestones and the current British Library record begin it at f. 10?
next_steps: Inspect a complete Sloane MS 73 facsimile or request confirmation from the British Library.
Check whether the discrepancy reflects recto-verso shorthand, historical foliation, or a simple error.

open_questions.question: Which exact Oxford University Press reprint or carrier copy did U-M encode?
next_steps: Ask U-M for the source-copy bibliographic record or scan.
Compare the XML title-page imprint and typography against dated EETS reprints.
notes: The XML SOURCEDESC names the 1889 N. Trübner revised edition, while its encoded title page has a later Oxford University Press London-New York-Toronto imprint without a reprint date.

open_questions.question: Was the revised edition's glossary deliberately excluded from the U-M item, or is the current XML incomplete?
next_steps: Compare against any alternate U-M delivery files or documentation.
Ask U-M whether page-range selection was intentional.

open_questions.question: Can complete authorized digital facsimiles of Sloane MS 73 and Harley MS 853 be obtained, and under what reuse terms?
next_steps: Submit British Library digitisation enquiries for complete-object photography and request quotations.
Request written publication and repository-reuse terms separately from image delivery.

notes: The current record is partial because the translation's precise origin, Latin exemplar, U-M carrier state, and complete-manuscript image availability remain unresolved.
Astrology is not assigned as a facet of the cataloged work: the astrological Spheres and Planets belongs to a separately appended related work.
Modern-English introduction, contents, and chemical notes are carrier paratext and therefore do not add English to the medieval work's catalog-summary language facet.
